dataset.name: NeuroimageTheoryBench
dataset.description: Pure neuroimaging knowledge QA tasks (no external dataset/tool-calling required)

tasks.title: NK-E-001
tasks.instruction: Reverse inference in neuroimaging refers to:

input.question: Reverse inference in neuroimaging refers to:

options.text: Inferring brain regions from observed cognitive processes
Inferring cognitive processes from observed brain activation
Reversing the hemodynamic response function in GLM
Using reverse correlation to identify relevant voxels

metadata.rationale: Tests basic definition vs common confusions (forward inference, technical terms)

tasks.title: NK-E-002
tasks.instruction: Which TWO factors are the PRIMARY inferential reasons reverse inference is risky in cognitive neuroscience? (Select TWO)

input.question: Which TWO factors are the PRIMARY inferential reasons reverse inference is risky in cognitive neuroscience? (Select TWO)

options.text: Most brain regions participate in multiple cognitive processes
Activation can reflect latent variables like difficulty or attention
BOLD signal-to-noise ratio is too low for reliable inference
fMRI cannot measure neural activity directly
Regions lack anatomical specificity due to smoothing

metadata.rationale: A and B are the primary inferential risks. C, D, and E are secondary or not specific to reverse inference.

tasks.title: NK-E-003
tasks.instruction: Which provides the STRONGEST evidence to reduce reverse inference risk?

input.question: Which provides the STRONGEST evidence to reduce reverse inference risk?

options.text: Larger sample size (N > 100)
Higher spatial resolution (< 2mm voxels)
Dissociation showing the region tracks the target process but not confounds
Replication in an independent dataset

metadata.rationale: A and D improve reliability but don't address specificity. B improves localization but not selectivity. Only C directly tests whether activation is diagnostic of the specific process.

tasks.title: NK-E-004
tasks.instruction: BOLD signal reflects: (Select all correct)

input.question: BOLD signal reflects: (Select all correct)

options.text: Changes in blood oxygenation
Direct measurement of neural spiking rate
Neurovascular coupling between metabolic demand and vascular response
Changes that can occur with increased inhibitory processing
A signal that varies by region's vascular anatomy

metadata.rationale: B is the key misconception to reject. Tests multi-faceted understanding.

tasks.title: NK-E-005
tasks.instruction: BOLD signal can INCREASE in all of the following scenarios EXCEPT:

input.question: BOLD signal can INCREASE in all of the following scenarios EXCEPT:

options.text: Increased excitatory synaptic activity
Increased inhibitory processing that raises metabolic demand
Decreased cerebral blood flow with constant metabolism
Increased synaptic activity without large changes in spiking

metadata.rationale: Decreased CBF would decrease BOLD. Tests understanding that BOLD is not uniquely tied to 'excitation.'

tasks.title: NK-E-006
tasks.instruction: Deactivation' in default mode network during a task operationally means:

input.question: Deactivation' in default mode network during a task operationally means:

options.text: Neurons in DMN regions stop firing
BOLD signal drops below zero (absolute deactivation)
BOLD signal for task condition is lower than the baseline in the GLM
Metabolic activity decreases relative to resting metabolism

metadata.rationale: Key point: it's model-relative, not absolute. D is a neurophysiological interpretation that may not be justified.

tasks.title: NK-E-007
tasks.instruction: Apparent DMN deactivation magnitude can be exaggerated by: (Select all)

input.question: Apparent DMN deactivation magnitude can be exaggerated by: (Select all)

options.text: Choice of baseline (rest vs active control)
Global signal regression
Respiratory fluctuations that interact with task structure
Motion differences between task and baseline
Low statistical power

metadata.rationale: E affects detection but not magnitude inflation. Tests understanding of nuisance/baseline issues.

tasks.title: NK-M-001
tasks.instruction: To distinguish prediction error from salience/surprise in model-based fMRI: (Select all necessary)

input.question: To distinguish prediction error from salience/surprise in model-based fMRI: (Select all necessary)

options.text: Use model comparison to test if prediction-error explains variance beyond salience proxies
Check collinearity between prediction-error regressor and RT or condition labels
Design tasks where prediction-error varies independently of stimulus intensity
Include orthogonal manipulations changing expected value without changing salience
Use higher spatial resolution to separate adjacent regions

metadata.rationale: E is irrelevant to computational specificity. Tests understanding of identifiability.

tasks.title: NK-M-002
tasks.instruction: You have strong RT differences between conditions. Deciding whether to include RT as covariate requires:

input.question: You have strong RT differences between conditions. Deciding whether to include RT as covariate requires:

options.text: Always include it to reduce noise
Never include it as it removes variance of interest
Determine if RT is a mediator or nuisance based on your causal model
Include it only if RT correlates with BOLD signal

metadata.rationale: Tests understanding that it changes the estimand. A and B are categorical errors, D is backwards reasoning.

tasks.title: NK-M-003
tasks.instruction: Above-chance decoding from early visual cortex shows:

input.question: Above-chance decoding from early visual cortex shows:

options.text: Participants consciously perceived the decoded stimulus dimension
Discriminable information exists in the measured patterns
The decoded dimension is represented in V1 neurons
The classifier has learned the ground truth neural code

metadata.rationale: Most conservative correct statement. A requires additional awareness measures. C and D are stronger claims than data support.

tasks.title: NK-M-004
tasks.instruction: Assume decoding is properly cross-validated and above chance. What additional evidence is needed to claim conscious awareness? (Select all)

input.question: Assume decoding is properly cross-validated and above chance. What additional evidence is needed to claim conscious awareness? (Select all)

options.text: Subjective awareness reports (e.g. visibility ratings)
Objective forced-choice measures under masking or threshold conditions
Control for low-level stimulus confounds (luminance/contrast)
Eye-tracking to rule out gaze confounds
Cross-validation to prevent overfitting

metadata.rationale: E is needed for valid decoding but doesn't address awareness specifically. Tests comprehensive control understanding.

tasks.title: NK-H-001
tasks.instruction: Functional connectivity (correlation between regional BOLD signals) primarily reflects:

input.question: Functional connectivity (correlation between regional BOLD signals) primarily reflects:

options.text: Direct anatomical connections between regions
Synchronized neural firing between regions
Statistical dependence that can arise from direct connections/indirect pathways/shared inputs
Information transfer between regions

metadata.rationale: Most defensible interpretation. A/B/D make causal/mechanistic claims not justified by correlation.

tasks.title: NK-H-002
tasks.instruction: Major confounds in resting-state functional connectivity include: (Select all)

input.question: Major confounds in resting-state functional connectivity include: (Select all)

options.text: Head motion creating spurious correlations
Respiratory and cardiac physiological fluctuations
Global signal variance (depending on GSR choice)
Scanner drift over time
Low spatial resolution reducing anatomical specificity

metadata.rationale: E affects localization but is not a 'confound' in the connectivity measure itself. Tests comprehensive confound knowledge.

tasks.title: NK-H-003
tasks.instruction: To claim a region 'implements' a computation (e.g. uncertainty), you need: (Select all)

input.question: To claim a region 'implements' a computation (e.g. uncertainty), you need: (Select all)

options.text: Correlation between BOLD and the computational regressor
The computational variable explains unique variance beyond RT/salience/condition
Cross-validated prediction showing the relationship generalizes
Dissociations via manipulations that change the computation without changing confounds
Evidence linking the neural signal to behavioral choices

metadata.rationale: A alone is insufficient (correlation ≠ implementation). Tests understanding of identifiability and linking hypotheses.

tasks.title: ME-E-001
tasks.instruction: To reduce susceptibility susceptibility artifacts (signal loss and geometric distortion) in OFC/MTL: (Select all effective)

input.question: To reduce susceptibility susceptibility artifacts (signal loss and geometric distortion) in OFC/MTL: (Select all effective)

options.text: Acquire reverse phase-encode pairs or fieldmaps for distortion correction
Shorten TE to reduce signal loss
Increase receiver bandwidth to reduce echo spacing
Use slice angulation to reduce through-plane field gradients
Increase voxel size to improve SNR in affected regions

metadata.rationale: E might improve SNR but doesn't address dropout mechanism. Tests understanding of multiple mitigation strategies.

tasks.title: ME-E-002
tasks.instruction: Multiband (SMS) acquisition provides: (Select all correct)

input.question: Multiband (SMS) acquisition provides: (Select all correct)

options.text: Shorter TR for better temporal sampling
Increased whole-brain coverage at a given TR
Reduced geometric distortion
Higher signal-to-noise ratio per volume
More volumes in fixed scan time (for better statistical power)

metadata.rationale: C is wrong (SMS doesn't directly affect in-plane distortion). D is wrong (actually may reduce tSNR due to g-factor).

tasks.title: ME-E-003
tasks.instruction: Potential quality risks with aggressive multiband acceleration: (Select all)

input.question: Potential quality risks with aggressive multiband acceleration: (Select all)

options.text: Slice leakage/aliasing artifacts
Increased g-factor noise reducing tSNR
Reconstruction artifacts
Reduced BOLD sensitivity due to faster sampling
Increased geometric distortion

metadata.rationale: D is not a direct SMS risk. E is wrong (SMS doesn't worsen distortion). Tests knowledge of SMS-specific artifacts.

tasks.title: ME-E-004
tasks.instruction: Reverse phase-encoding ('blip up/down') images are acquired to:

input.question: Reverse phase-encoding ('blip up/down') images are acquired to:

options.text: Measure B0 field inhomogeneity directly
Estimate susceptibility distortion by comparing opposite warps
Improve signal-to-noise ratio through averaging
Validate scanner gradient calibration

metadata.rationale: A describes fieldmap. C and D are incorrect purposes. Tests understanding of the principle.

tasks.title: ME-E-005
tasks.instruction: Which statement BEST defines the difference between coregistration and normalization?

input.question: Which statement BEST defines the difference between coregistration and normalization?

options.text: Coregistration is linear; normalization is nonlinear
Coregistration aligns functional to subject anatomy; normalization warps to template space
Coregistration is for within-subject; normalization is for between-subject
Coregistration preserves resolution; normalization resamples data

metadata.rationale: B is the defining distinction. A/C/D may occur in practice but are not the core definition.

tasks.title: ME-E-006
tasks.instruction: Confusing coregistration and normalization can cause:

input.question: Confusing coregistration and normalization can cause:

options.text: Longer processing time but correct results
Pipeline crashes with error messages
Silent mislocalization and invalid spatial correspondence
Reduced statistical power only

metadata.rationale: Key point: 'the pipeline may run without errors while silently producing invalid spatial correspondence.'

tasks.title: ME-E-007
tasks.instruction: Why record respiratory and cardiac signals in fMRI? (Select all correct)

input.question: Why record respiratory and cardiac signals in fMRI? (Select all correct)

options.text: These signals introduce structured BOLD fluctuations
They can alias into task-relevant frequencies
Recording allows physiological noise regressors (e.g. RETROICOR)
Short TR eliminates physiological noise so recording is optional
Physiology may differ by group or session creating confounds

metadata.rationale: D is false - short TR improves sampling but doesn't eliminate physio. Tests multi-reason understanding.

tasks.title: ME-M-001
tasks.instruction: At 3T, gray matter T2* ≈ 30ms. Optimal TE for BOLD sensitivity is:

input.question: At 3T, gray matter T2* ≈ 30ms. Optimal TE for BOLD sensitivity is:

options.text: 10ms
20ms
30ms
60ms

metadata.rationale: Direct application of TE ≈ T2* principle. Tests concrete parameter knowledge.

tasks.title: ME-M-002
tasks.instruction: You might deviate from TE ≈ T2* to: (Select all valid reasons)

input.question: You might deviate from TE ≈ T2* to: (Select all valid reasons)

options.text: Reduce dropout in susceptibility-prone regions (shorter TE)
Accommodate readout/echo spacing constraints
Support multi-echo acquisition for improved robustness
Increase BOLD sensitivity (longer TE)
Match literature protocols for comparability

metadata.rationale: D is backwards - longer TE beyond T2* reduces sensitivity. Tests understanding of tradeoffs.

tasks.title: ME-M-003
tasks.instruction: Protocol A: TR=2s, 3mm, no SMS. Protocol B: TR=0.8s, 2mm, SMS=4. For rapid event-related designs, Protocol B primarily offers:

input.question: Protocol A: TR=2s, 3mm, no SMS. Protocol B: TR=0.8s, 2mm, SMS=4. For rapid event-related designs, Protocol B primarily offers:

options.text: Higher per-volume SNR
Better temporal sampling and efficiency for rapid events
Simpler preprocessing and reconstruction
Lower artifact risk

metadata.rationale: A and C favor Protocol A. D is ambiguous. Tests understanding of short-TR benefits.

tasks.title: ME-M-004
tasks.instruction: Preprocessing completes successfully but FA is globally low (<0.2) and tractography is unstable. Most likely cause:

input.question: Preprocessing completes successfully but FA is globally low (<0.2) and tractography is unstable. Most likely cause:

options.text: Participant has severe white matter disease
Incorrect bvec/bval handling (axis flips/sign errors/wrong units)
Too few diffusion directions
Eddy current correction failed

metadata.rationale: Global pattern + 'completed successfully' suggests parameter error not algorithm failure. Tests diagnostic reasoning.

tasks.title: ME-M-005
tasks.instruction: In-plane geometric distortion in EPI primarily scales with:

input.question: In-plane geometric distortion in EPI primarily scales with:

options.text: TR (repetition time)
Total readout time / echo spacing
Multiband acceleration factor
Voxel size

metadata.rationale: A affects temporal sampling. C affects TR/coverage. D affects resolution. Only B directly determines distortion.

tasks.title: ME-M-006
tasks.instruction: To minimize geometric distortion while maintaining whole-brain coverage and reasonable TR:

input.question: To minimize geometric distortion while maintaining whole-brain coverage and reasonable TR:

options.text: Increase multiband factor (reduces distortion directly)
Increase bandwidth and in-plane acceleration (reduces readout time)
Decrease voxel size (improves resolution reduces distortion)
Increase TE (allows more time for distortion correction)

metadata.rationale: A helps TR/coverage but doesn't reduce in-plane distortion. C worsens readout time. D is backwards.

tasks.title: ME-M-007
tasks.instruction: Visible Nyquist ghosting in EPI that varies over time can:

input.question: Visible Nyquist ghosting in EPI that varies over time can:

options.text: Be ignored as cosmetic if it doesn't overlap task regions
Create structured temporal variance correlating with motion/physiology
Be fixed by increasing receiver bandwidth
Indicate successful distortion correction

metadata.rationale: Time-varying ghosting is NOT cosmetic. Tests understanding of temporal artifact impacts.

tasks.title: ME-H-001
tasks.instruction: What is the BEST explanation for 'vein dominance' in gradient-echo BOLD?

input.question: What is the BEST explanation for 'vein dominance' in gradient-echo BOLD?

options.text: It measures venous blood flow not arterial input
It is sensitive to susceptibility changes around draining veins spatially displacing activation
Veins have higher BOLD signal than tissue
It cannot distinguish arteries from veins anatomically

metadata.rationale: B captures susceptibility effects around draining veins that can spatially displace activation; other options are incomplete or mechanism-mismatched.

tasks.title: ME-H-002
tasks.instruction: To reduce macrovascular (large vessel) contribution to BOLD using established approaches: (Select all)

input.question: To reduce macrovascular (large vessel) contribution to BOLD using established approaches: (Select all)

options.text: Use spin-echo EPI instead of gradient-echo
Increase spatial resolution to reduce partial voluming with veins
Use VASO (vascular space occupancy) contrast
Increase multiband factor
Use shorter TE

metadata.rationale: D affects TR/coverage. E reduces SNR but doesn't specifically target microvasculature.

tasks.title: ME-H-003
tasks.instruction: Session effects in longitudinal fMRI can arise from: (Select all non-neural)

input.question: Session effects in longitudinal fMRI can arise from: (Select all non-neural)

options.text: Changes in shim quality affecting dropout
Coil loading/positioning differences
Hardware or software upgrades
Participant physiological state changes (hydration/caffeine)
Learning effects on task performance

metadata.rationale: E is a neural/behavioral effect not a scanner confound. Tests comprehensive confound knowledge.

tasks.title: ME-H-004
tasks.instruction: Prospective motion correction offers advantages over post-hoc realignment because it:

input.question: Prospective motion correction offers advantages over post-hoc realignment because it:

options.text: Provides more accurate motion parameter estimates
Reduces motion during acquisition limiting spin-history and motion-by-susceptibility effects
Eliminates the need for motion regressors in GLM
Works better for slow drifts than sudden movements

metadata.rationale: A is not the key advantage. C is false (still need regressors). D is backwards.

tasks.title: PP-E-001
tasks.instruction: Task-correlated motion spikes remain after 6-parameter motion correction because: (Select all)

input.question: Task-correlated motion spikes remain after 6-parameter motion correction because: (Select all)

options.text: Rigid-body parameters don't capture spin-history effects
Motion-by-susceptibility interactions are not modeled
Six parameters are insufficient for micro-movements
Motion correction always fails for task-correlated motion
The motion parameters need to be z-scored first

metadata.rationale: D is false. E is irrelevant. Tests understanding of why 6 params are insufficient.

tasks.title: PP-E-002
tasks.instruction: Standard next steps to reduce task-correlated motion artifacts: (Select all)

input.question: Standard next steps to reduce task-correlated motion artifacts: (Select all)

options.text: Add motion derivatives and squares to GLM
Use spike regressors or censoring for high-motion frames
Apply ICA-based denoising
Increase smoothing kernel
Check if effects localize to edges/CSF (artifact signature)

metadata.rationale: D doesn't remove motion artifacts just spreads them. Tests standard mitigation strategies.

tasks.title: PP-E-003
tasks.instruction: Functional-to-anatomical alignment looks good globally but fails in OFC/temporal poles. Most likely reason:

input.question: Functional-to-anatomical alignment looks good globally but fails in OFC/temporal poles. Most likely reason:

options.text: OFC anatomy is highly variable across subjects
Severe EPI distortion and dropout near air-tissue interfaces
Insufficient smoothing in those regions
Wrong template space selected

metadata.rationale: Local failure in predictable regions = susceptibility. A doesn't explain alignment failure. C and D are irrelevant.

tasks.title: PP-E-004
tasks.instruction: FD alone is insufficient for QC because it can miss which additional artifact classes? (Select all that apply)

input.question: FD alone is insufficient for QC because it can miss which additional artifact classes? (Select all that apply)

options.text: FD misses intensity spikes not due to motion
FD doesn't detect physiological noise
FD doesn't catch registration or mask failures
FD threshold is arbitrary and varies by study
FD doesn't indicate whether dropout/ghosting is present

metadata.rationale: A/B/C/E are artifact classes not captured by FD alone. D is about threshold policy, not a missed artifact class.

tasks.title: PP-E-005
tasks.instruction: Assume stimulus onset is precisely time-locked to scanner triggers and you are running an event-related design. Slice-timing correction is LEAST impactful when:

input.question: Assume stimulus onset is precisely time-locked to scanner triggers and you are running an event-related design. Slice-timing correction is LEAST impactful when:

options.text: TR is long (>2s) and design is event-related
TR is very short (<1s) due to multiband
Stimulus presentation is not time-locked to scanner
Using block designs

metadata.rationale: Short TR → small timing offsets relative to sampling rate. Tests understanding of when STC matters.

tasks.title: PP-E-006
tasks.instruction: The core scientific tradeoff of spatial smoothing is:

input.question: The core scientific tradeoff of spatial smoothing is:

options.text: Processing time vs result quality
Increased SNR/detectability vs reduced spatial specificity
Improved normalization vs worse coregistration
Better correction of motion vs worse correction of distortion

metadata.rationale: A is practical not scientific. C and D are unrelated to smoothing's effect.

tasks.title: PP-M-001
tasks.instruction: When reporting results with GSR in resting-state you should: (Select all)

input.question: When reporting results with GSR in resting-state you should: (Select all)

options.text: Note that GSR removes global shared variance that may include neural activity
Provide sensitivity analyses with and without GSR
State that GSR can induce artificial anticorrelations
Report motion/physio QC and group differences
Claim that GSR-induced anticorrelations reflect true neural architecture

metadata.rationale: E is backwards - should interpret cautiously. Tests comprehensive GSR reporting.

tasks.title: PP-M-002
tasks.instruction: Filtering data before regression but not filtering regressors causes:

input.question: Filtering data before regression but not filtering regressors causes:

options.text: Improved noise reduction
Biased coefficient estimation
Faster computation with identical results
Better removal of low-frequency drift

metadata.rationale: Inconsistent filtering creates bias. Tests understanding of filter-regression order.

tasks.title: PP-M-003
tasks.instruction: To decide whether to remove an ICA component that resembles DMN: (Select all diagnostics)

input.question: To decide whether to remove an ICA component that resembles DMN: (Select all diagnostics)

options.text: Check overlap with edge/CSF/vascular masks
Inspect temporal frequency spectrum for spikes
Correlate with FD/DVARS and physio regressors
Check stability across runs
Always keep components resembling canonical networks

metadata.rationale: E is a categorical error. Tests diagnostic reasoning.

tasks.title: PP-M-004
tasks.instruction: Motion causes distance-dependent connectivity bias primarily by:

input.question: Motion causes distance-dependent connectivity bias primarily by:

options.text: Moving the head closer to or farther from the coil
Mixing signals across voxels and introducing global structured fluctuations
Changing the effective distance between regions in template space
Causing longer fibers to move more than shorter fibers

metadata.rationale: A affects SNR not connectivity pattern. C and D are spatial not signal effects.

tasks.title: PP-M-005
tasks.instruction: ROI results change substantially with different normalization settings. This indicates:

input.question: ROI results change substantially with different normalization settings. This indicates:

options.text: The analysis is reproducible and robust
Sensitivity to spatial correspondence; requires QC of alignment and ROI placement
Normalization worked correctly
The ROI is in a high-variability region and should be excluded

metadata.rationale: Pipeline-dependence is a robustness concern. Tests interpretation of sensitivity.

tasks.title: PP-M-006
tasks.instruction: Surface-based analysis is MOST beneficial when: (Select all)

input.question: Surface-based analysis is MOST beneficial when: (Select all)

options.text: Studying cortical regions with complex sulcal/gyral folding
You want to improve anatomical correspondence across subjects in cortex
Reducing partial-volume effects at sulcal boundaries
Analyzing subcortical structures (striatum/thalamus)
Reducing preprocessing time

metadata.rationale: D is wrong - surfaces don't help subcortex. E is wrong - often slower.

tasks.title: PP-H-001
tasks.instruction: When comparing groups with different motion levels, the PRIMARY tradeoff between motion-matching vs covarying is:

input.question: When comparing groups with different motion levels, the PRIMARY tradeoff between motion-matching vs covarying is:

options.text: Motion-matching preserves sample size; covarying reduces it
Motion-matching reduces confounding but may reduce representativeness; covarying assumes correct model
Motion-matching is always preferable if feasible
Covarying is more conservative than matching

metadata.rationale: A is backwards. C and D are too categorical. Tests nuanced understanding.

tasks.title: PP-H-002
tasks.instruction: Best practice for group differences with unequal motion: (Select all)

input.question: Best practice for group differences with unequal motion: (Select all)

options.text: Show results with strong motion modeling in full sample
Repeat analysis in motion-matched subsample
Report FD/DVARS distributions by group
Check for artifact signatures (edge/CSF localization)
If results differ between approaches acknowledge this as a limitation

metadata.rationale: Comprehensive robustness requires all. Tests best-practice knowledge.

tasks.title: PP-H-003
tasks.instruction: Surface-based analysis primarily improves:

input.question: Surface-based analysis primarily improves:

options.text: SNR in subcortical regions
Anatomical correspondence in cortex by respecting sulcal/gyral topology
Processing speed for large datasets
Correction for head motion

metadata.rationale: A is false (surfaces don't help subcortex). C and D are unrelated.

tasks.title: PP-H-004
tasks.instruction: When using surface-based analysis with clinical groups: (Select all pitfalls)

input.question: When using surface-based analysis with clinical groups: (Select all pitfalls)

options.text: Surface reconstruction errors due to poor tissue contrast or lesions
Group differences in cortical thickness affecting surface topology
Failures to generate surfaces for subjects with atrophy or structural abnormalities
Surface analysis automatically handling all group anatomical differences
Surfaces being incompatible with group analysis

metadata.rationale: D is backwards. E is wrong. A/B/C are real clinical QC concerns.

tasks.title: PP-H-005
tasks.instruction: In diffusion MRI preprocessing, topup and eddy have distinct roles:

input.question: In diffusion MRI preprocessing, topup and eddy have distinct roles:

options.text: topup corrects motion; eddy corrects distortion
topup estimates B0 field from reverse-PE images; eddy corrects eddy currents/motion/outliers
topup is for single-shell; eddy is for multi-shell
They are interchangeable tools

metadata.rationale: A is backwards. C and D are false. Tests tool-specific knowledge.

tasks.title: PP-H-006
tasks.instruction: Wrong phase-encoding direction in topup parameters causes:

input.question: Wrong phase-encoding direction in topup parameters causes:

options.text: Failed convergence with error message
Warp estimated/applied in wrong direction producing stretched anatomy
Slightly reduced correction quality
No effect if motion correction is applied

metadata.rationale: Silent failure producing wrong output. Tests awareness of parameter criticality.

tasks.title: ST-E-001
tasks.instruction: Ignoring temporal autocorrelation in fMRI GLM residuals causes:

input.question: Ignoring temporal autocorrelation in fMRI GLM residuals causes:

options.text: Biased parameter estimates (incorrect beta values)
Underestimated standard errors and inflated false positives
Overestimated standard errors and reduced power
No effect if sample size is large enough

metadata.rationale: Autocorrelation violates independence leading to underestimated SEs (not overestimated). Parameter estimates remain unbiased.

tasks.title: ST-E-002
tasks.instruction: A study (N=200) reports 'significant activation' (p=0.001) in dlPFC. This means:

input.question: A study (N=200) reports 'significant activation' (p=0.001) in dlPFC. This means:

options.text: The effect is large and clinically meaningful
There is strong evidence against the null hypothesis given the sample size and noise
The result will replicate in future studies
The dlPFC activation is necessary for the task

metadata.rationale: P-value measures evidence against null not magnitude. Doesn't guarantee replication or imply causality/necessity.

tasks.title: ST-E-003
tasks.instruction: When reporting fMRI results, you should include: (Select all correct)

input.question: When reporting fMRI results, you should include: (Select all correct)

options.text: Only p-values as they summarize all relevant information
Effect sizes (e.g. percent signal change/Cohen's d)
Confidence intervals or standard errors for effect sizes
Enough information for power calculations in future studies
Multiple comparison correction method only if results are significant

metadata.rationale: A is wrong (p-values alone insufficient). E is wrong (should always report correction method).

tasks.title: ST-E-004
tasks.instruction: Mixed-effects models for group fMRI analysis account for:

input.question: Mixed-effects models for group fMRI analysis account for:

options.text: Only within-subject variability
Only between-subject variability
Both within-subject estimates and between-subject variability
Variability in scanner hardware across sites

metadata.rationale: A describes fixed-effects. B is incomplete. D is a confound not what mixed-effects models.

tasks.title: ST-E-005
tasks.instruction: A contrast 'Condition A > Condition B' showing significant activation means:

input.question: A contrast 'Condition A > Condition B' showing significant activation means:

options.text: Condition A absolutely activates the region (BOLD > 0)
Condition B shows no neural engagement
The estimated BOLD signal for A is greater than B under the specified model
Condition A is necessary for activating this region

metadata.rationale: Contrasts are relative comparisons not absolute. B doesn't follow from A>B. D implies causality/necessity.

tasks.title: ST-E-006
tasks.instruction: p > 0.05 in an ROI analysis indicates:

input.question: p > 0.05 in an ROI analysis indicates:

options.text: The region is not involved in the task
The null hypothesis is true
Insufficient evidence to reject the null possibly due to low power or noise
The effect size is exactly zero

metadata.rationale: Non-significance ≠ no effect. Can't prove null true from p>0.05. D is too strong.

tasks.title: ST-M-001
tasks.instruction: You preregistered a confirmatory family (whole-brain + 10 ROIs). You also ran 15 brain-behavior correlations labeled as exploratory. The MOST defensible multiplicity correction strategy is:

input.question: You preregistered a confirmatory family (whole-brain + 10 ROIs). You also ran 15 brain-behavior correlations labeled as exploratory. The MOST defensible multiplicity correction strategy is:

options.text: Correct for all tests combined using a single Bonferroni correction
Correct within the confirmatory family; report exploratory analyses separately with appropriate correction and replication plan
Only correct within whole-brain; ROIs and correlations are hypothesis-driven
No correction needed if hypotheses were preregistered

metadata.rationale: A mixes confirmatory and exploratory obscuring inference levels. C is too liberal. D is wrong.

tasks.title: ST-M-002
tasks.instruction: Which scenario represents double dipping?

input.question: Which scenario represents double dipping?

options.text: Using whole-brain analysis to identify a region then testing it in an independent dataset
Selecting an ROI based on peak activation in Contrast A then testing Contrast A in that ROI
Using anatomical masks defined from a brain atlas
Running both voxel-wise and cluster-wise corrections as sensitivity analyses

metadata.rationale: B is classic double-dipping. A uses independent data. C uses independent criterion. D is sensitivity analysis.

tasks.title: ST-M-003
tasks.instruction: Valid strategies to avoid double dipping in ROI analysis: (Select all)

input.question: Valid strategies to avoid double dipping in ROI analysis: (Select all)

options.text: Use independent anatomical ROIs defined from atlas
Use independent functional localizer from separate runs/sessions
Split-half: select ROI on half the data test on the other half
Use smaller ROIs to reduce circularity
Increase sample size to make the bias negligible

metadata.rationale: D doesn't eliminate circularity. E doesn't fix the bias. A/B/C all use independent data.

tasks.title: ST-M-004
tasks.instruction: Your model-based regressor correlates 0.7 with condition labels and 0.5 with RT. Signs of problematic collinearity include: (Select all)

input.question: Your model-based regressor correlates 0.7 with condition labels and 0.5 with RT. Signs of problematic collinearity include: (Select all)

options.text: Unstable coefficient estimates (large changes with small data perturbations)
High variance inflation factors (VIF > 5-10)
Reduced overall model fit (lower R²)
Difficulty attributing shared variance to specific regressors
Increased false positive rate in whole-brain analysis

metadata.rationale: C is wrong (collinearity doesn't reduce R² just makes coefficients unstable). E is not a direct consequence.

tasks.title: ST-M-005
tasks.instruction: Task t-statistics dropped after adding 24 motion parameters + CompCor. The PRIMARY diagnostic to determine if you removed artifact vs over-controlled signal is:

input.question: Task t-statistics dropped after adding 24 motion parameters + CompCor. The PRIMARY diagnostic to determine if you removed artifact vs over-controlled signal is:

options.text: Re-run without any motion correction to see if effects return
Check collinearity between task and nuisance regressors inspect spatial patterns for artifact signatures
Increase smoothing to recover lost signal
Use more nuisance regressors to ensure complete artifact removal

metadata.rationale: A creates confound. C doesn't address root issue. D worsens potential over-control.

tasks.title: ST-M-006
tasks.instruction: To make cluster-based inference transparent and reproducible, you should report: (Select all)

input.question: To make cluster-based inference transparent and reproducible, you should report: (Select all)

options.text: The cluster-forming threshold used
Smoothness estimation method and correction procedure
Sensitivity analyses across reasonable thresholds OR complementary voxel-wise correction
Only the threshold that yields significant results without disclosing other tested thresholds
Cluster results exclusively as voxel-wise results add no value

metadata.rationale: D is selective reporting and lacks transparency. E dismisses valuable complementary evidence.

tasks.title: ST-M-007
tasks.instruction: Filtering fMRI data before nuisance regression, but NOT filtering the nuisance regressors, causes:

input.question: Filtering fMRI data before nuisance regression, but NOT filtering the nuisance regressors, causes:

options.text: Improved noise reduction
Biased coefficient estimates for nuisance regressors
Faster computation with identical results
Better removal of low-frequency drift

metadata.rationale: Inconsistent filtering creates bias in regression. A/C/D are wrong.

tasks.title: ST-M-008
tasks.instruction: Permutation tests are MOST valuable in fMRI when:

input.question: Permutation tests are MOST valuable in fMRI when:

options.text: You want faster computation than parametric tests
Parametric assumptions (e.g. normality/smoothness) are questionable or sample size is small
You need to inflate p-values to be more conservative
You want to avoid multiple comparison correction

metadata.rationale: A is wrong (permutation is computationally expensive). C is wrong (doesn't inflate provides empirical null). D is wrong.

tasks.title: ST-M-009
tasks.instruction: You want to control false positives while maintaining reasonable power for exploratory whole-brain analysis. Between FWE (family-wise error) and FDR (false discovery rate), the MOST appropriate choice is:

input.question: You want to control false positives while maintaining reasonable power for exploratory whole-brain analysis. Between FWE (family-wise error) and FDR (false discovery rate), the MOST appropriate choice is:

options.text: Always use FWE because it's more conservative
FDR, because it controls the proportion of false positives among discoveries
Either one, they control the same thing
Neither, use uncorrected p < 0.001

metadata.rationale: FDR is less conservative than FWE better for exploratory discovery. A is too rigid. C is wrong. D is inadequate.

tasks.title: ST-M-010
tasks.instruction: A result survives FDR q < 0.05 but not FWE p < 0.05. This means:

input.question: A result survives FDR q < 0.05 but not FWE p < 0.05. This means:

options.text: The result is definitely a false positive
Among all discoveries at this threshold, the expected false positive rate is ≤5%
The probability this specific result is false is exactly 5%
The result would survive FWE with a larger sample size

metadata.rationale: FDR controls expected proportion of false discoveries not individual probability. A is too strong. C confuses FDR with posterior probability.

tasks.title: ST-M-011
tasks.instruction: Type S (sign) error refers to:

input.question: Type S (sign) error refers to:

options.text: Rejecting the null when it's true (false positive)
Failing to reject the null when it's false (false negative)
Finding a statistically significant effect in the wrong direction
Finding the correct direction but wrong magnitude

metadata.rationale: Type S = sign error. A is Type I error. B is Type II error. D is Type M error.

tasks.title: ST-M-012
tasks.instruction: Type M (magnitude) error is MOST likely when:

input.question: Type M (magnitude) error is MOST likely when:

options.text: Sample size is very large
True effect size is large relative to noise
Study is underpowered (low power small true effect)
Multiple comparisons correction is not applied

metadata.rationale: Underpowered studies that reach significance tend to overestimate effect magnitude. A and B reduce Type M error.

tasks.title: ST-H-001
tasks.instruction: You normalized features using full dataset mean/variance before cross-validation. This is problematic because:

input.question: You normalized features using full dataset mean/variance before cross-validation. This is problematic because:

options.text: Normalization should be done after cross-validation not before
Test-fold information influences training through global normalization parameters
Cross-validation requires standardization not normalization
Full dataset normalization reduces model accuracy

metadata.rationale: Information leakage from test to train. A is imprecise. C confuses terms. D is consequence not reason.

tasks.title: ST-H-002
tasks.instruction: To properly avoid leakage in cross-validated classification: (Select all necessary)

input.question: To properly avoid leakage in cross-validated classification: (Select all necessary)

options.text: Fit normalization parameters on training folds only then apply to test fold
Perform feature selection within each training fold (not on full data)
Use nested cross-validation if tuning hyperparameters
Use leave-one-out CV instead of k-fold CV to eliminate leakage
Estimate normalization parameters using the combined train+test data within each fold

metadata.rationale: D doesn't prevent leakage. E is explicit leakage - using test data in normalization.

tasks.title: ST-H-003
tasks.instruction: You report 85% accuracy on the same dataset used for feature selection and hyperparameter tuning. What is the SINGLE most important statistical issue?

input.question: You report 85% accuracy on the same dataset used for feature selection and hyperparameter tuning. What is the SINGLE most important statistical issue?

options.text: Sample size is too small for high-dimensional data
Optimistic bias from evaluating on non-independent data (information leakage)
85% is too high to be believable for brain data
Need to use regularization to prevent overfitting

metadata.rationale: B identifies optimistic bias from non-independent evaluation (information leakage); other options are secondary or remedial.

tasks.title: ST-H-004
tasks.instruction: If an external held-out dataset is available, the gold-standard evaluation setup for high-dimensional brain-behavior prediction is:

input.question: If an external held-out dataset is available, the gold-standard evaluation setup for high-dimensional brain-behavior prediction is:

options.text: 10-fold cross-validation with feature selection and tuning inside each fold
Nested cross-validation (tuning in inner folds evaluation in outer folds)
External held-out dataset completely independent from any model development
Bootstrap resampling with 1000 iterations

metadata.rationale: C is gold standard when available. B is best internal validation but C is superior. A has potential leakage.

tasks.title: ST-H-005
tasks.instruction: To make a causal mediation claim (condition → brain signal → behavior) in fMRI, you must assume: (Select all necessary)

input.question: To make a causal mediation claim (condition → brain signal → behavior) in fMRI, you must assume: (Select all necessary)

options.text: Correct temporal/causal ordering: condition precedes brain signal precedes behavior
No unmeasured confounders of the brain signal → behavior relationship
No mediator-outcome confounders that are themselves affected by the condition
Brain signal is measured with acceptable reliability (not severe measurement error)
The relationships are perfectly linear with no interactions

metadata.rationale: E is too strong - mediation analysis can accommodate nonlinearity. A/B/C/D are core assumptions.

tasks.title: ST-H-006
tasks.instruction: To claim 'no meaningful group difference in BOLD response', you must:

input.question: To claim 'no meaningful group difference in BOLD response', you must:

options.text: Show p > 0.05 with N > 100
Show the 95% CI includes zero
Define SESOI (smallest effect of interest) and show the 95% CI falls entirely within [-SESOI +SESOI]
Show Bayes factor favors null with BF₀₁ > 3

metadata.rationale: C is equivalence testing with pre-defined SESOI (gold standard). A and B are absence of evidence. D is acceptable but C is more complete.

tasks.title: ST-H-007
tasks.instruction: In an underpowered fMRI study (power = 20%) that finds a 'significant' effect, you should be concerned about: (Select all)

input.question: In an underpowered fMRI study (power = 20%) that finds a 'significant' effect, you should be concerned about: (Select all)

options.text: Type S error (effect might be in the wrong direction)
Type M error (effect size likely overestimated sometimes dramatically)
The result is unlikely to replicate at the reported magnitude
The p-value is incorrectly calculated
The result is definitely a false positive

metadata.rationale: Low power → high Type S/M error rates when significant. D is wrong - p-value calculation independent of power. E is too strong.

tasks.title: ST-H-008
tasks.instruction: A Bayes Factor BF₁₀ = 0.1 (or BF₀₁ = 10) means:

input.question: A Bayes Factor BF₁₀ = 0.1 (or BF₀₁ = 10) means:

options.text: The null hypothesis is true
The data are 10 times more likely under the null than the alternative
There is a 10% probability the alternative is true
The p-value would be 0.10

metadata.rationale: BF is likelihood ratio not posterior probability or p-value. A is too strong. C confuses BF with posterior.

tasks.title: ST-H-009
tasks.instruction: ROPE (Region of Practical Equivalence) in Bayesian analysis is used to:

input.question: ROPE (Region of Practical Equivalence) in Bayesian analysis is used to:

options.text: Define a range of effect sizes considered negligible for practical purposes
Calculate the Bayes Factor automatically
Replace confidence intervals
Determine the optimal sample size

metadata.rationale: ROPE defines 'practically zero' effect region. B is wrong - ROPE and BF are different. C is wrong - complementary to credible intervals.

tasks.title: TR-E-001
tasks.instruction: Group activation maps show bright clusters along brain edges and ventricles rather than plausible cortical regions. The MOST LIKELY cause is:

input.question: Group activation maps show bright clusters along brain edges and ventricles rather than plausible cortical regions. The MOST LIKELY cause is:

options.text: Strong neural activation in periventricular regions
Motion/physiological artifacts creating edge/CSF fluctuations correlated with task
Insufficient smoothing failing to detect deep brain structures
Normal pattern for subcortical activation

metadata.rationale: Edge/ventricle localization is classic artifact signature. A is biologically implausible. C is backwards. D mischaracterizes pattern.

tasks.title: TR-E-002
tasks.instruction: Given edge/ventricle artifacts in group maps, the first diagnostic checks are: (Select all)

input.question: Given edge/ventricle artifacts in group maps, the first diagnostic checks are: (Select all)

options.text: Verify brain/WM/CSF masks are anatomically correct
Check FD/DVARS and test whether spike regressors or censoring removes the pattern
Increase spatial smoothing to 10mm FWHM
Re-run analysis with different statistical threshold
Switch to surface-based analysis

metadata.rationale: A and B directly diagnose artifact source. C doesn't diagnose just spreads artifact. D changes detection not diagnosis.

tasks.title: TR-E-003
tasks.instruction: Mean EPI shows a half-FOV shifted duplicate 'ghost' of the brain. This artifact is:

input.question: Mean EPI shows a half-FOV shifted duplicate 'ghost' of the brain. This artifact is:

options.text: Susceptibility dropout from field inhomogeneity
Nyquist (N/2) ghosting from odd/even k-space mismatch
Motion-induced blurring
Successful distortion correction creating a reference image

metadata.rationale: Half-FOV shift is characteristic of Nyquist ghosting. A causes dropout not ghosting. C creates blur not ghost.

tasks.title: TR-E-004
tasks.instruction: Time-varying Nyquist ghosting is problematic because it:

input.question: Time-varying Nyquist ghosting is problematic because it:

options.text: Reduces spatial resolution uniformly across the image
Creates structured temporal variance that can correlate with motion/task timing
Is purely cosmetic and can be ignored if it doesn't overlap task ROIs
Indicates successful preprocessing

metadata.rationale: Time-varying ghosting becomes structured noise in timeseries. A describes static resolution loss. C is dangerously wrong.

tasks.title: TR-E-005
tasks.instruction: Functional brain masks incorrectly including skull/air in some subjects will corrupt: (Select all)

input.question: Functional brain masks incorrectly including skull/air in some subjects will corrupt: (Select all)

options.text: Functional-to-anatomical registration quality
WM/CSF nuisance regressors (contaminated by non-brain signal)
ROI extraction and group-level spatial mapping
Slice-timing correction accuracy
Task design timing

metadata.rationale: D is not affected by masks (slice-timing is temporal mask-independent). E is completely unrelated to image processing.

tasks.title: TR-E-006
tasks.instruction: DVARS spikes occur without corresponding FD spikes. This MOST LIKELY indicates:

input.question: DVARS spikes occur without corresponding FD spikes. This MOST LIKELY indicates:

options.text: Head motion is occurring but FD is insensitive to it
Intensity changes not captured by rigid motion (physiological spikes/scanner instability/spin-history)
DVARS threshold is set too conservatively
Successful motion correction removed the motion artifacts

metadata.rationale: DVARS without FD = intensity changes beyond rigid motion. A is backwards. C is about threshold not interpretation.

tasks.title: TR-M-001
tasks.instruction: After distortion correction, EPI looks unwarped for some subjects but stretched in the wrong direction for others. The MOST COMMON root cause is:

input.question: After distortion correction, EPI looks unwarped for some subjects but stretched in the wrong direction for others. The MOST COMMON root cause is:

options.text: Subject head size variability affecting distortion magnitude
Incorrect phase-encoding direction sign or total readout time in parameters
Fieldmap quality varying across subjects
Different brain anatomy requiring different correction strategies

metadata.rationale: Wrong PE direction → warp applied in wrong direction. A affects magnitude uniformly not direction. C affects quality not systematic wrong-direction.

tasks.title: TR-M-002
tasks.instruction: To verify distortion correction parameters are correct: (Select all necessary checks)

input.question: To verify distortion correction parameters are correct: (Select all necessary checks)

options.text: Confirm phase-encoding axis and polarity match actual acquisition
Verify total readout time and echo spacing are correct
Check that reverse-PE or fieldmap scans match EPI acquisition parameters
Ensure all subjects have identical brain anatomy
Verify scanner software version is consistent across sessions

metadata.rationale: D is biologically impossible and irrelevant. E is QC but not parameter verification. A/B/C are critical parameter checks.

tasks.title: TR-M-003
tasks.instruction: Eddy reports many outliers and slice dropouts; tractography is unstable. Before discarding subjects, you should FIRST check:

input.question: Eddy reports many outliers and slice dropouts; tractography is unstable. Before discarding subjects, you should FIRST check:

options.text: Whether subjects have underlying white matter pathology
Raw DWI quality, bvec/bval orientation and units, and eddy/topup parameter specification
Tractography algorithm parameters and seed density
Whether diffusion model order is too high (causing overfitting)

metadata.rationale: Most unstable tractography is parameter/metadata errors not biology. A is premature. C addresses symptoms. D is unrelated.

tasks.title: TR-M-004
tasks.instruction: Strong left-right intensity asymmetry in mean EPI across many subjects is MOST LIKELY due to:

input.question: Strong left-right intensity asymmetry in mean EPI across many subjects is MOST LIKELY due to:

options.text: Genuine hemispheric lateralization of neural activity
Coil sensitivity profiles, bias field, or distortion patterns (artifact)
Subjects consistently turning their heads to one side
Template brain being asymmetric

metadata.rationale: Systematic asymmetry across subjects = artifact not biology. A is implausible. C would show in motion parameters.

tasks.title: TR-M-005
tasks.instruction: To diagnose left-right intensity asymmetry: (Select all appropriate checks)

input.question: To diagnose left-right intensity asymmetry: (Select all appropriate checks)

options.text: Check bias-field correction quality and receive coil profiles
Verify coverage is symmetric across hemispheres
Inspect distortion patterns and phase-encoding direction effects
Increase smoothing to average across hemispheres
Assume it reflects true biological asymmetry and proceed

metadata.rationale: D doesn't diagnose just hides problem. E is dangerous assumption. A/B/C all diagnose potential artifact sources.

tasks.title: TR-M-006
tasks.instruction: Low tSNR in a subset of runs but motion (FD) is not elevated. Plausible causes include: (Select all)

input.question: Low tSNR in a subset of runs but motion (FD) is not elevated. Plausible causes include: (Select all)

options.text: Scanner instability or drift
Physiological noise (respiratory/cardiac) fluctuations
Time-varying ghosting artifacts
Poor shimming or partial coverage issues
Subject anatomical differences

metadata.rationale: E doesn't cause run-to-run tSNR differences within subject. A/B/C/D all can reduce tSNR independent of head motion.

tasks.title: TR-M-007
tasks.instruction: Activation clusters 'move' spatially between runs within the same subject. The MOST LIKELY pipeline mistake is:

input.question: Activation clusters 'move' spatially between runs within the same subject. The MOST LIKELY pipeline mistake is:

options.text: Different statistical thresholds applied to different runs
Space mixing (some runs in native space others in template space)
Different task timing across runs
Subject learning effects changing activation patterns

metadata.rationale: Spatial displacement = space/transform error not statistical or neural. A changes detection not location. C/D would change magnitude/extent not location.

tasks.title: TR-M-008
tasks.instruction: FreeSurfer labeled dura as cortex in multiple subjects. The MOST COMMON preprocessing cause is:

input.question: FreeSurfer labeled dura as cortex in multiple subjects. The MOST COMMON preprocessing cause is:

options.text: Subjects have unusually thick cortex
Skull-stripping failure and/or inadequate bias-field correction
Wrong FreeSurfer version for this age group
Template brain mismatch

metadata.rationale: Dura inclusion = brain mask too large from poor skull-stripping. A is biology not preprocessing error. C/D unlikely to cause systematic dura labeling.

tasks.title: TR-H-001
tasks.instruction: One subject drives the group effect; removing them flips the conclusion. The scientifically correct approach is:

input.question: One subject drives the group effect; removing them flips the conclusion. The scientifically correct approach is:

options.text: Remove the subject because they are an outlier
Keep the subject because exclusion is data manipulation
Use pre-defined objective QC criteria; report results with and without the subject; acknowledge fragility
Keep the subject only if their data support your hypothesis

metadata.rationale: Transparent handling with objective criteria is correct. A requires justification beyond effect direction. B is too rigid. D is p-hacking.

tasks.title: TR-H-002
tasks.instruction: Reviewers suspect motion-driven distance bias in your connectivity group differences. To test this, you should: (Select all)

input.question: Reviewers suspect motion-driven distance bias in your connectivity group differences. To test this, you should: (Select all)

options.text: Plot edge-distance dependence for each group
Correlate connectivity metrics with FD/DVARS
Test whether effects persist after motion matching and stronger denoising
Increase distance threshold to include only long-range connections
Remove all short-range connections from analysis

metadata.rationale: D and E modify analysis without diagnosing artifact. A/B/C all diagnose whether motion explains pattern.

tasks.title: TR-H-003
tasks.instruction: Which pattern would MOST reassure you that group connectivity differences are not motion artifacts?

input.question: Which pattern would MOST reassure you that group connectivity differences are not motion artifacts?

options.text: Long-distance edges show stronger effects than short-distance edges
Effect remains similar after motion matching; doesn't track FD/DVARS; not concentrated in motion-sensitive edges
Effect becomes stronger after motion censoring
Effect appears only in cortical regions not subcortex

metadata.rationale: B shows robustness to motion confound. A is consistent with motion artifact. C suggests motion contamination. D doesn't address motion.

tasks.title: TR-H-004
tasks.instruction: After preprocessing, some subjects have NaN or extreme outliers in ROI timeseries, but the pipeline didn't crash. Silent causes include: (Select all)

input.question: After preprocessing, some subjects have NaN or extreme outliers in ROI timeseries, but the pipeline didn't crash. Silent causes include: (Select all)

options.text: Applying ROI masks in the wrong space (mask-data mismatch)
Zero-variance voxels from dropout within the ROI
Interpolation outside brain after misregistration
Bad brain masks including non-brain voxels in the ROI
Subject moving too much during the scan

metadata.rationale: E would be caught by QC metrics not 'silent'. A/B/C/D all create numerical issues without pipeline errors.

tasks.title: TR-H-005
tasks.instruction: To locate the source of NaN values in ROI timeseries, the MOST SYSTEMATIC diagnostic approach is:

input.question: To locate the source of NaN values in ROI timeseries, the MOST SYSTEMATIC diagnostic approach is:

options.text: Re-run the entire pipeline with different software
Overlay ROI masks on functional data in final analysis space; check voxel variance; trace transform chain
Exclude affected subjects as poor data quality
Increase smoothing to fill in missing values

metadata.rationale: B systematically diagnoses spatial and numerical issues. A is brute-force without diagnosis. C is premature. D hides problem.

tasks.title: TR-H-006
tasks.instruction: To diagnose scanner drift separate from participant motion in longitudinal data: (Select all)

input.question: To diagnose scanner drift separate from participant motion in longitudinal data: (Select all)

options.text: Track mean signal and tSNR over sessions using phantom scans
Compare drift in stationary regions (deep WM) versus motion metrics (FD)
Examine frequency spectra for unusual low-frequency trends
Increase high-pass filter cutoff to remove drift
Exclude all sessions with any detectable drift

metadata.rationale: D treats symptoms without diagnosis. E is overly strict. A/B/C all diagnose scanner vs subject contributions.

tasks.title: IN-E-001
tasks.instruction: An fMRI contrast shows 'Region X activation in Condition A > Condition B'. The MOST accurate interpretation is:

input.question: An fMRI contrast shows 'Region X activation in Condition A > Condition B'. The MOST accurate interpretation is:

options.text: Region X uniquely implements the cognitive process in Condition A
Region X is necessary for Condition A but not Condition B
The model-estimated BOLD signal for A is greater than B under the specified preprocessing and baseline
Condition B produces no neural activity in Region X

metadata.rationale: Contrasts show relative model-estimated differences not absolute or causal claims. A/B/D are common over-inferences.

tasks.title: IN-E-002
tasks.instruction: A significant contrast 'A > B' does NOT demonstrate: (Select all)

input.question: A significant contrast 'A > B' does NOT demonstrate: (Select all)

options.text: That Region X uniquely processes Condition A
That Region X is necessary for Condition A
A causal relationship between Region X and behavior
That the statistical test was properly conducted
That preprocessing was appropriate

metadata.rationale: A requires specificity evidence. B requires necessity evidence like lesion/TMS. C requires causal manipulation. D/E are about methodology.

tasks.title: IN-E-003
tasks.instruction: Deactivation' in a brain region during a task operationally means:

input.question: Deactivation' in a brain region during a task operationally means:

options.text: Neurons in that region stop firing completely
Metabolic activity drops to zero
BOLD signal is lower than the chosen baseline/reference condition in the GLM
The region is damaged or non-functional

metadata.rationale: Deactivation is baseline-relative not absolute. A/B imply literal shutdown. D confuses statistical result with pathology.

tasks.title: IN-E-004
tasks.instruction: When reporting 'deactivation', the MOST scientifically accurate phrasing is:

input.question: When reporting 'deactivation', the MOST scientifically accurate phrasing is:

options.text: The region was deactivated during the task
Neural activity decreased relative to rest
BOLD signal was lower during task than baseline, under our model and nuisance controls
The region was not involved in the task

metadata.rationale: C acknowledges model-dependence and baseline-relativity. A implies absolute deactivation. B makes neural claim from hemodynamic measure. D overclaims.

tasks.title: IN-E-005
tasks.instruction: Functional connectivity (correlation between regional BOLD timeseries) primarily indicates:

input.question: Functional connectivity (correlation between regional BOLD timeseries) primarily indicates:

options.text: Direct anatomical connections between regions
Causal information flow from one region to another
Statistical dependence that can arise from multiple sources (direct/indirect connections, shared inputs)
Synchronized neural firing between regions

metadata.rationale: Correlation = statistical dependence not mechanism. A requires anatomical tracing. B requires causal analysis. D requires electrophysiology.

tasks.title: IN-E-006
tasks.instruction: A null result (p > 0.05) in an ROI means:

input.question: A null result (p > 0.05) in an ROI means:

options.text: The region is definitively not involved in the task
The null hypothesis is proven true
Insufficient evidence to reject the null; could reflect low power, noise, or model mismatch
The effect size is exactly zero

metadata.rationale: Non-significance ≠ no effect. A and B conflate absence of evidence with evidence of absence. D is too strong.

tasks.title: IN-M-001
tasks.instruction: The same region activates in Task A (working memory) and Task B (cognitive control). The MOST scientifically defensible interpretation is:

input.question: The same region activates in Task A (working memory) and Task B (cognitive control). The MOST scientifically defensible interpretation is:

options.text: The region implements both working memory and cognitive control
Tasks A and B actually engage the same cognitive process
Overlap is weak evidence; regions are multifunctional and tasks share latent variables
The region is the neural substrate for executive function

metadata.rationale: Overlap alone is weak due to multifunctionality and shared demands. A/B/D overclaim implementation/equivalence/substrate.

tasks.title: IN-M-002
tasks.instruction: To strengthen claims beyond simple overlap, you need: (Select all)

input.question: To strengthen claims beyond simple overlap, you need: (Select all)

options.text: Orthogonal or parametric manipulations showing the region tracks a specific variable across tasks
Dissociations showing the region responds to target process but not confounds
Predictive generalization of the pattern across contexts
Larger cluster size in the overlap region
Higher statistical threshold to ensure significance

metadata.rationale: D is about detection not interpretive strength. E is about statistical stringency not mechanistic evidence. A/B/C provide specificity.

tasks.title: IN-M-003
tasks.instruction: Above-chance decoding of stimulus category from visual cortex demonstrates:

input.question: Above-chance decoding of stimulus category from visual cortex demonstrates:

options.text: Conscious perception of the decoded dimension
The region causally generates the decoded representation
Discriminable information exists in the measured patterns
The decoded dimension is represented at the neuronal level

metadata.rationale: Decoding = information is discriminable not consciousness or mechanism. A requires awareness measures. B requires causal manipulation. D is stronger than data support.

tasks.title: IN-M-004
tasks.instruction: Assume decoding is properly cross-validated and above chance. To claim the decoded information reflects conscious awareness, you additionally need: (Select all)

input.question: Assume decoding is properly cross-validated and above chance. To claim the decoded information reflects conscious awareness, you additionally need: (Select all)

options.text: Subjective awareness reports (e.g. visibility ratings)
Objective forced-choice under masking or threshold conditions
Control for low-level stimulus confounds (luminance/contrast/spatial frequency)
Eye-tracking to rule out gaze position confounds
Higher decoding accuracy (>90%)

metadata.rationale: E is about magnitude not awareness per se. A and B directly measure awareness. C and D rule out alternative explanations.

tasks.title: IN-M-005
tasks.instruction: Clinical group shows higher BOLD activation than controls. Before claiming neural differences, consider: (Select all alternative explanations)

input.question: Clinical group shows higher BOLD activation than controls. Before claiming neural differences, consider: (Select all alternative explanations)

options.text: Greater task difficulty or effort in the clinical group
Performance differences (accuracy/RT) between groups
Motion or physiological artifact differences
Vascular reactivity differences (medication, baseline physiology)
Different task strategies between groups

metadata.rationale: All five are plausible non-neural explanations for BOLD differences. Tests comprehensive confound awareness.

tasks.title: IN-M-006
tasks.instruction: To interpret hyperactivation as 'compensation' rather than artifact or inefficiency, you MUST show:

input.question: To interpret hyperactivation as 'compensation' rather than artifact or inefficiency, you MUST show:

options.text: Activation magnitude is statistically significantly higher in patients
Higher activation relates to maintained or improved performance within the clinical group
The region is larger in patients than controls
Patients have been diagnosed for a longer duration

metadata.rationale: Compensation requires performance relationship. A is necessary but not sufficient. C is anatomical not functional. D is duration not performance.

tasks.title: IN-M-007
tasks.instruction: You observe decreased resting-state connectivity after an intervention. The MOST scientifically accurate framing is:

input.question: You observe decreased resting-state connectivity after an intervention. The MOST scientifically accurate framing is:

options.text: Information transfer between regions decreased
Neural communication was disrupted by the intervention
Connectivity estimates changed under our preprocessing and nuisance model
The intervention damaged anatomical pathways

metadata.rationale: C acknowledges correlation and model-dependence. A/B imply mechanistic transfer/communication. D implies structural damage from functional measure.

tasks.title: IN-M-008
tasks.instruction: You found a strong brain-behavior correlation after exploring many ROIs and behavioral metrics. To keep it in the paper defensibly, you should:

input.question: You found a strong brain-behavior correlation after exploring many ROIs and behavioral metrics. To keep it in the paper defensibly, you should:

options.text: Present it as a confirmatory finding since it's highly significant
Label it exploratory, describe the search space, apply appropriate correction, and propose replication
Move it to the main results if the p-value is below 0.001
Remove all mention of other tested ROIs and metrics

metadata.rationale: Exploratory findings require transparency and replication. A conflates significance with confirmatory status. C uses p-value inappropriately. D is selective reporting.

tasks.title: IN-H-001
tasks.instruction: BOLD group differences could reflect: (Select all)

input.question: BOLD group differences could reflect: (Select all)

options.text: Neural activity differences
Vascular reactivity differences (vessel compliance, baseline CBF)
Medication effects on neurovascular coupling
Differential motion or SNR between groups
Only neural differences, as BOLD is a direct neural measure

metadata.rationale: E is fundamentally wrong - BOLD is hemodynamic. A/B/C/D all can cause BOLD differences. Tests understanding BOLD is ambiguous.

tasks.title: IN-H-002
tasks.instruction: To strengthen the interpretation of BOLD group differences as neural (not vascular), you could: (Select all)

input.question: To strengthen the interpretation of BOLD group differences as neural (not vascular), you could: (Select all)

options.text: Include vascular calibration (breath-hold, CO2 challenge)
Add perfusion measures (ASL) to assess baseline CBF
Compare motion/physiology QC metrics between groups
Show convergent evidence from behavior or other modalities
Use higher statistical threshold to ensure significance

metadata.rationale: E is about detection stringency not vascular/neural disambiguation. A/B directly address vascular. C addresses artifact. D provides convergent validity.

tasks.title: IN-H-003
tasks.instruction: To claim a region 'implements' a computation (e.g. uncertainty estimation), beyond simple correlation with a model regressor, you need: (Select all)

input.question: To claim a region 'implements' a computation (e.g. uncertainty estimation), beyond simple correlation with a model regressor, you need: (Select all)

options.text: The computational variable explains unique variance beyond alternatives (RT/salience/condition)
Model comparison showing your computational model fits better than alternatives
Dissociations via manipulations that change the computation without changing confounds
Evidence linking the neural signal to behavioral choices or parameter generalization
Activation magnitude correlation with task difficulty

metadata.rationale: E could reflect difficulty confound not computational specificity. A/B/C/D address identifiability/validity/confounds/relevance.

tasks.title: IN-H-004
tasks.instruction: Your main effect appears only with one preprocessing choice (e.g. only with GSR, not without). The MOST appropriate interpretation and reporting strategy is:

input.question: Your main effect appears only with one preprocessing choice (e.g. only with GSR, not without). The MOST appropriate interpretation and reporting strategy is:

options.text: Report only the pipeline that shows the effect
Report the effect as robust since it appears in at least one pipeline
Report results under both pipelines; explain estimand changes; justify primary choice; acknowledge fragility
Average results across pipelines to get a consensus estimate

metadata.rationale: Pipeline-dependence indicates fragility requiring transparency. A is selective reporting. B overclaims robustness. D is statistically inappropriate.

tasks.title: IN-H-005
tasks.instruction: Your behavioral effect replicates across labs but the fMRI effect does not. The MOST scientifically defensible interpretation is:

input.question: Your behavioral effect replicates across labs but the fMRI effect does not. The MOST scientifically defensible interpretation is:

options.text: The original fMRI finding was a false positive
Neural effects are less reliable; non-replication could reflect scanner/protocol/pipeline differences or insufficient power
fMRI is not a valid method for studying this phenomenon
The replication attempt was poorly conducted

metadata.rationale: B acknowledges multiple explanations without overclaiming. A is too strong without power/methods analysis. C overclaims method invalidity. D is defensive.

tasks.title: IN-H-006
tasks.instruction: Causal language about brain-behavior relationships (e.g. 'Region X generates behavior Y') is MOST justified when:

input.question: Causal language about brain-behavior relationships (e.g. 'Region X generates behavior Y') is MOST justified when:

options.text: The correlation is statistically significant with p < 0.001
The effect size is large (Cohen's d > 0.8)
You have causal manipulation (TMS, lesion, optogenetics) or strong time-lagged prediction
The finding replicates in multiple independent datasets

metadata.rationale: Only causal manipulation or strong temporal precedence justifies causal claims. A is about statistical evidence. B is about magnitude. D is about robustness.

tasks.title: BP-E-001
tasks.instruction: For another lab to reproduce your neuroimaging analysis, you must report: (Select all minimum requirements)

input.question: For another lab to reproduce your neuroimaging analysis, you must report: (Select all minimum requirements)

options.text: Acquisition parameters (TR, TE, resolution, phase-encoding direction)
Preprocessing steps with software versions
Full model specification (design matrix, contrasts, nuisance regressors)
Multiple-comparisons correction method
QC criteria and exclusion rules

metadata.rationale: All five are minimum requirements for reproducibility. Omitting any makes exact replication impossible.

tasks.title: BP-E-002
tasks.instruction: The MOST COMMON missing detail that breaks reproducibility in fMRI papers is:

input.question: The MOST COMMON missing detail that breaks reproducibility in fMRI papers is:

options.text: Acquisition parameters (TR, TE)
Sample size and demographics
Exact nuisance model and preprocessing parameter settings
Statistical threshold used

metadata.rationale: Nuisance model details most often omitted. A/B/D usually reported. 'Standard preprocessing' without exact parameters is insufficient.

tasks.title: BP-E-003
tasks.instruction: The difference between data QC and inference QC is:

input.question: The difference between data QC and inference QC is:

options.text: Data QC is for raw data; inference QC is for processed data
Data QC verifies measurement validity; inference QC verifies statistical validity
Data QC is automated; inference QC is manual
They are the same thing with different names

metadata.rationale: Data QC = artifacts/alignment/masks; Inference QC = assumptions/multiplicity/leakage. A is imprecise. C is incorrect. D is wrong.

tasks.title: BP-E-004
tasks.instruction: Your team says 'let's try different pipelines and pick the one with strongest activation in expected regions.' This is problematic because:

input.question: Your team says 'let's try different pipelines and pick the one with strongest activation in expected regions.' This is problematic because:

options.text: Different pipelines should never be compared
It uses outcome to select method, inflating false positives and reinforcing confirmation bias
Only one pipeline can ever be correct for a given dataset
It takes too much computational time

metadata.rationale: Outcome-based selection is circular and inflates Type I error. A is too strong. C is wrong. D is practical not scientific.

tasks.title: BP-E-005
tasks.instruction: To avoid 'space mixing' errors (native vs template vs surface) in a multi-step pipeline, the MOST effective strategy is:

input.question: To avoid 'space mixing' errors (native vs template vs surface) in a multi-step pipeline, the MOST effective strategy is:

options.text: Use only one space throughout the entire analysis
Explicit space labeling in filenames/metadata and QC snapshots after each transform
Always normalize to template space as the first step
Rely on software defaults to handle spaces automatically

metadata.rationale: Explicit labeling and verification prevents silent errors. A is too restrictive. C doesn't prevent errors. D is dangerous.

tasks.title: BP-E-006
tasks.instruction: When reporting fMRI results, you should include behavioral performance: (Select all necessary)

input.question: When reporting fMRI results, you should include behavioral performance: (Select all necessary)

options.text: Accuracy and RT distributions by condition
Whether behavior differs by condition or group
Explanation of whether behavioral differences are part of the construct or a confound
Only if the study is primarily behavioral
Only if performance correlates with brain activity

metadata.rationale: D is wrong - always report behavior in task fMRI. E is wrong - report regardless of correlation. A/B/C all necessary.

tasks.title: BP-M-001
tasks.instruction: When preregistering an fMRI analysis, the PRIMARY distinction between confirmatory and exploratory is:

input.question: When preregistering an fMRI analysis, the PRIMARY distinction between confirmatory and exploratory is:

options.text: Confirmatory analyses use higher statistical thresholds
Confirmatory hypotheses, contrasts, and ROIs are specified before seeing data; exploratory are not
Confirmatory analyses must use parametric tests
Exploratory analyses cannot be published

metadata.rationale: Pre-specification is the defining feature. A is about stringency. C is about test type. D is wrong.

tasks.title: BP-M-002
tasks.instruction: A preregistration for fMRI should include: (Select all)

input.question: A preregistration for fMRI should include: (Select all)

options.text: Primary hypotheses and specific contrasts to test
ROI definitions (anatomical or functional localizer approach)
Model specification including planned nuisance regressors and filtering
Multiple-comparisons correction method
QC criteria and exclusion thresholds

metadata.rationale: All are essential to lock down confirmatory analysis. Omitting any allows garden of forking paths.

tasks.title: BP-M-003
tasks.instruction: You have 10 ROIs and 5 behavioral measures. To avoid 'garden of forking paths' while remaining useful for theory, the BEST strategy is:

input.question: You have 10 ROIs and 5 behavioral measures. To avoid 'garden of forking paths' while remaining useful for theory, the BEST strategy is:

options.text: Test all 50 combinations and correct for 50 comparisons
Predefine 2-3 primary brain-behavior hypotheses with correction; transparently report exploration for the rest
Only test combinations that yield p < 0.05
Report only the strongest correlation from each ROI

metadata.rationale: Separates confirmatory (corrected) from exploratory (transparent). A is overly conservative. C and D are selective reporting/p-hacking.

tasks.title: BP-M-004
tasks.instruction: The PRIMARY purpose of sensitivity analyses (e.g. with/without GSR, different motion thresholds) is to:

input.question: The PRIMARY purpose of sensitivity analyses (e.g. with/without GSR, different motion thresholds) is to:

options.text: Find the pipeline that gives the strongest effects
Demonstrate robustness of findings across reasonable analytic choices
Satisfy reviewers who request additional analyses
Increase the number of significant results
