metadata.rationale: Sensitivity analyses test robustness not maximize significance. A is outcome-based selection. C is superficial. D is p-hacking.

tasks.title: BP-M-005
tasks.instruction: To prevent 'it ran on my machine' reproducibility failures when sharing code: (Select all)

input.question: To prevent 'it ran on my machine' reproducibility failures when sharing code: (Select all)

options.text: Use version control (git) with tagged releases
Capture environment (containers or dependency lockfiles)
Document exact software versions and parameters
Include README with data layout assumptions and a test case
Require users to have identical hardware

metadata.rationale: E is impossible and unnecessary. A/B/C/D are all standard reproducibility practices.

tasks.title: BP-M-006
tasks.instruction: To ensure QC-based exclusions are defensible and not biased, you should:

input.question: To ensure QC-based exclusions are defensible and not biased, you should:

options.text: Exclude any subject whose data looks unusual
Predefine objective QC thresholds before seeing group assignments or results
Exclude subjects only from the control group to match clinical group quality
Never exclude any subjects regardless of quality

metadata.rationale: Pre-defined objective criteria prevent biased exclusion. A is subjective. C is biased. D is too rigid.

tasks.title: BP-M-007
tasks.instruction: When comparing groups with different motion levels, best practice reporting includes: (Select all)

input.question: When comparing groups with different motion levels, best practice reporting includes: (Select all)

options.text: FD/DVARS distributions for each group
Results with strong motion modeling in full sample
Results in motion-matched subsample
Censoring/scrubbing rates by group
Exclude the high-motion group entirely

metadata.rationale: E is extreme and loses data. A/B/C/D provide comprehensive transparency.

tasks.title: BP-M-008
tasks.instruction: Your conclusion changes substantially with different normalization settings. You should:

input.question: Your conclusion changes substantially with different normalization settings. You should:

options.text: Report only the normalization that shows the effect
Report both settings; justify primary choice (ideally pre-specified); acknowledge fragility; recommend replication
Average across normalization methods
Claim the effect is robust since it appears in at least one pipeline

metadata.rationale: Pipeline-dependence requires transparency and caution. A is selective reporting. C is statistically inappropriate. D overclaims robustness.

tasks.title: BP-H-001
tasks.instruction: You tried five preprocessing variants. The MOST defensible reporting approach to avoid p-hacking while enabling exploration is:

input.question: You tried five preprocessing variants. The MOST defensible reporting approach to avoid p-hacking while enabling exploration is:

options.text: Report only the pre-specified primary pipeline
Report the pipeline with strongest effects as the main result
Predefine primary pipeline for confirmatory claims; report sensitivity across all variants with QC metrics; validate key findings on held-out data
Report whichever pipeline a reviewer requests

metadata.rationale: C separates confirmation/transparency/validation. A hides fragility. B is outcome-based. D is reactive not principled.

tasks.title: BP-H-002
tasks.instruction: When reporting results across multiple pipelines, include: (Select all)

input.question: When reporting results across multiple pipelines, include: (Select all)

options.text: Primary results from pre-specified or QC-justified pipeline
Sensitivity table showing effects across all reasonable variants
QC metrics (not just p-values) for each pipeline
Held-out or replication validation for headline claims if pipeline was selected post-hoc
Only pipelines that yield significant results

metadata.rationale: E is selective reporting. A/B/C/D provide structured transparency.

tasks.title: BP-H-003
tasks.instruction: To prevent leakage/memorization in a neuroimaging benchmark using published literature: (Select all necessary)

input.question: To prevent leakage/memorization in a neuroimaging benchmark using published literature: (Select all necessary)

options.text: Near-duplicate detection to remove paraphrased content
Time-based holdouts (test on papers after model training cutoff)
Remove distinctive phrasing that might have been memorized
Test for exposure effects (performance on older/highly-cited material)
Include only unpublished data

metadata.rationale: E is impractical and unnecessary. A/B/C/D are all concrete leakage controls.

tasks.title: BP-H-004
tasks.instruction: Before claiming clinical relevance from a neuroimaging prediction model, you must: (Select all minimum requirements)

input.question: Before claiming clinical relevance from a neuroimaging prediction model, you must: (Select all minimum requirements)

options.text: Strict train-test independence (nested CV for tuning, ideally external validation)
Report calibration and uncertainty, not just accuracy
Use appropriate baselines (not just chance)
Assess and report confound sensitivity (motion, site effects)
Report class balance and demonstrate leakage controls

metadata.rationale: All five are minimum standards for clinical claims. Omitting any undermines validity.

tasks.title: BP-H-005
tasks.instruction: The PRIMARY purpose of multiverse analysis (testing all reasonable analytic combinations) is to:

input.question: The PRIMARY purpose of multiverse analysis (testing all reasonable analytic combinations) is to:

options.text: Find the pipeline that maximizes effect size
Demonstrate which analytic choices matter most for the conclusion
Generate more publishable results
Replace the need for replication

metadata.rationale: Multiverse reveals analytic dependence/robustness. A is outcome optimization. C misunderstands purpose. D is wrong.

tasks.title: BP-H-006
tasks.instruction: When facing unavoidable researcher degrees of freedom (multiple defensible preprocessing choices), the approach that BEST balances rigor and discovery is:

input.question: When facing unavoidable researcher degrees of freedom (multiple defensible preprocessing choices), the approach that BEST balances rigor and discovery is:

options.text: Lock down one pipeline and never deviate
Try all options and report only what works
Transparently separate confirmatory (pre-specified, corrected) from exploratory (reported with sensitivity, requiring replication)
Let reviewers decide which pipeline to use

metadata.rationale: C uses transparency and hypothesis hierarchies. A is too rigid. B is p-hacking. D abdicates responsibility.
